root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

direct sums

Up to isomorphism, the space of mixed tensors $\bbr^{2 \ 3}{}_{4}$ is a triply-indexed direct sum of $24$ copies of $\bbr$.

the direct sum existence theorem

Recall way back in the first post, that I motivated the binary tensor product $\otimes$ by “inventing” it as a way to multiply vectors $v\in V$ and $w\in W$, which may come from different vector spaces. To make this work, a third vector space $V \otimes W$ was invented that serves as the target for this new multiplication, in the sense that the binary tensor product of vectors defines a map

\begin{equation} \label{times-map-eqn} V \times W \to V \otimes W, \quad (v,w) \mapsto v \otimes w. \end{equation}

Moreover, this new invented product, $\otimes$, has several natural properties encoded in the bilinearity relations. But what if we wanted to add the vectors $v$ and $w$ instead? Could we “invent” a way to do this, in the same spirit as our invention of the tensor product?

Yes!

Suppose that some sort of hypothetical addition of $v$ and $w$ exists, which we denote $v \oplus w$, and call the direct sum of $v$ and $w$. What sort of properties would we want it to have?

First, we need to decide what sort of vector the direct sum $v\oplus w$ is. Where does it live? Back in $V$ or $W$? As I just mentioned, this was the exact problem that we faced when considering the tensor product $v\otimes w$ (where does it live?), and to resolve it, we invented the new vector space $V \otimes W$ to contain these products. We shall do the same thing here, by inventing a new vector space $V \oplus W$, which we call the direct sum of $V$ and $W$, in order to obtain an “addition” map

\begin{equation} \label{addition-map-eqn} V \times W \to V \oplus W, \quad (v,w) \mapsto v \oplus w, \end{equation}

in complete analogy with \eqref{times-map-eqn}.

The properties that we shall require of our new direct sum can be encoded in the equations

\begin{equation} \label{defining-eqn} (v+v’) \oplus (w + w’) = (v\oplus w) + (v’ \oplus w’) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation} \label{defining2-eqn} (cv) \oplus (cw) = c(v\oplus w), \end{equation}

for all $v,v’ \in V$, $w,w’\in W$, and $c\in \bbr$. Take care to note that the single symbol $+$ occurs three times in the equation \eqref{defining-eqn}, and it stands for three different additions. Indeed, the addition $v+v’$ on the left-hand side is happening in $V$, the addition $w+w’$ is happening in $W$, while the addition on the right-hand side of the equation is the vector addition in $V\oplus W$. The equations \eqref{defining-eqn} roughly say that these three additions interact coherently, and not in some random, haphazard fashion. The second set of defining equations \eqref{defining2-eqn} have a similar interpretation: notice that the scalings $cv$ and $cw$ are happening in $V$ and $W$, respectively, while the scaling on the right-hand side is happening in $V\oplus W$. Thus, these equations say that these three different scalar multiplications interact coherently.

The relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} are the “additive analogs” of the bilinearity relations for the tensor product, in the sense that they completely characterize the direct sum, just as the bilinearity relations completely characterized the tensor product. When we discussed the tensor product, we deduced the bilinearity relations by assuming that the tensor product exists, and then writing down the natural properties that it ought to have. However, we still needed to appeal to an existence theorem in order to conclude that the tensor product exists! The situation here with the direct sum is similar: though we have deduced the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} as properties that the direct sum ought to have, we haven’t actually shown that the direct sum exists. However, in complete analogy with the Tensor Existence Theorem, we have:

Direct Sum Existence Theorem. Both the vector space $V \oplus W$ and the addition $\oplus$ exist. More precisely, the map \eqref{addition-map-eqn} exists, and it has the following properties:

  1. It is surjective.
  2. The relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} hold.
  3. No other relations besides \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn} hold.

As a consequence, the addition map \eqref{addition-map-eqn} is an isomorphism of vector spaces.

Note that the Direct Sum Existence Theorem says more about direct sums than the Tensor Existence Theorem says about tensor products. Indeed, the latter simply says that tensor products exist, but it doesn’t give you an explicit construction for the tensor product (you’d have to look for it in the proof). In contrast, the Direct Sum Existence Theorem gives you an explicit model for the direct sum, namely the cartesian product $V\times W$. Thus, you would be quite correct to identify $V\oplus W$ with $V\times W$, and this is the definition often given for $V\oplus W$ in many sources.

You might ask: why didn’t I simply define the direct sum $V\oplus W$ as $V\times W$, and avoid this extended discussion?

My goal is to shift the emphasis from any particular model of the direct sum, like the cartesian product, to what’s really important: the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn}. The whole point of the direct sum is that it allows us to add vectors that live in different vector spaces, and it does so in as “natural” a way as possible, without any extraneous properties or relations. We want to cook up a way to make sense of $v\oplus w$, but we don’t particularly care exactly how $v\oplus w$ is constructed, just as long as it has the natural properties that we want (and no others).

Taken a step further, there’s a school of thought in theoretical mathematics that systematically de-emphasizes static, model-level properties and constructions of objects in favor of defining and manipulating objects via their dynamic, “morphism-level” properties. In other words, what’s important about a mathematical object is not the particularities and specificities of any one of its constructions, but rather how it interacts with all the other objects in its category. This is (one of) the main lessons in category theory.

So, to summarize, while my approach to the direct sum is not exactly the one taken in category theory—for that, I would have had to explain biprodcuts—it is still categorical in spirit, since it shifts attention away from the construction of $V\oplus W$ as a cartesian product in favor of the relations \eqref{defining-eqn} and \eqref{defining2-eqn}.

properties of the direct sum

The vector spaces $V$ and $W$ may be embedded in the direct sum $V\oplus W$, allowing us to view a vector $v$ as either living in $V$, or the direct sum $V\oplus W$. Indeed, to accomplish this, we agree to write

\begin{equation} \label{convention-eqn} v = v \oplus 0_W \quad \text{and} \quad w = 0_V \oplus w, \end{equation}

where $0_V$ and $0_W$ are the zero vectors (i.e., the additive identities) in $V$ and $W$. Be sure to understand that these equations are not literally true, and are merely conventions that we are agreeing upon! Using these identifications, and by taking $w=w’=0_W$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get that

\begin{equation} \label{v-eqn} v + v’ = v+v’. \end{equation}

This equation appears to have no content, but remember that the addition on the left-hand side is in $V$, while the addition on the right-hand side is in $V\oplus W$. Similarly, by taking $v=v’=0_V$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get

\begin{equation} \label{w-eqn} w + w’ = w+w’. \end{equation}

Taken together, these last two displayed equations show that the addition in $V\oplus W$ extends the pre-existing additions in $V$ and $W$. A third interesting consequence of the conventions \eqref{convention-eqn} is that, if we take $v’=0_V$ and $w’ = 0_W$ in \eqref{defining-eqn}, we get

\begin{equation} \label{oplus-eqn} v \oplus w = v+ w. \end{equation}

This shows that the direct sum operation $\oplus$ may be identified with the vector space addition in $V\oplus W$.

Essentially, all of these identifications justify us writing strings of additions of vectors in $V$ and $W$ like

\begin{equation} \label{string-eqn} v_1 + w_1 + v_2 + v_3 + w_2 + w_3 \in V\oplus W, \end{equation}

where the $v$’s are in $V$ and the $w$’s are in $W$. The identifications \eqref{oplus-eqn} tell us that, in this string, whenever we see an addition like $v_\ast + w_\ast$, that that’s really the direct sum $v_\ast \oplus w_\ast$; the identifications \eqref{v-eqn} tell us that, when we see something like $v_\ast + v_{\ast}$, with two vectors in $v$ being added, that that’s really an addition in $V$; and finally, whenever we see $w_\ast + w_\ast$, the convention \eqref{w-eqn} tells us that that’s really an addition in $W$. The moral is that strings like \eqref{string-eqn} are written all the time, and that if you dig deep enough, you can make precise sense out of them; however, I encourage you to take strings like this at face value, and manipulate them just like you would any other sum, and only dig deeper into their meaning if you are absolutely forced to!

abstract indices and direct sums

We could, if we really wanted to, go through a careful construction of direct sums of more than two vectors spaces. Such things would look like

\begin{equation} \label{linear-eqn} V^1 \oplus V^2 \oplus \cdots \oplus V^p. \end{equation}

However, at the end of the day, we think of this $p$-fold direct sum as consisting of nothing but strings of additions like \eqref{string-eqn} of vectors from the $V$’s. For example, inside the $3$-fold direct sum $V \oplus W \oplus U$, I could write a string like

\begin{equation}\notag v_1 + v_2 + u_1 + v_3 + w_1 + u_2 + w_2, \end{equation}

and if I really wanted to, I could carefully take it apart and say precisely what each of the additions really mean. But we won’t do this! Instead, we will just assume that strings like this make sense, with the knowledge that we could carefully explain them if we were forced to.

The direct sum \eqref{linear-eqn} is indexed using a single index, by which I mean that

\begin{equation} \label{linear2-eqn} \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^p V^\alpha = V^1 \oplus V^2 \oplus \cdots \oplus V^p. \end{equation}

We will use a single abstract upper index to represent a vector in this direct sum, so instead of writing $v$ to stand for a vector in \eqref{linear2-eqn}, we will write $v^a$. Precisely, this means that

\begin{equation}\notag v^a = \sum_{\alpha=1}^p \iota_\alpha(v^\alpha), \end{equation}

where $v^\alpha\in V^\alpha$ is the $\alpha$-th component of $v$ and where summation over repeated indices is implied. Moreover, we shall write $v^\alpha \iota_\alpha$ to stand for $\iota_\alpha(v^\alpha)$, so that we can write

\begin{equation}\notag v^a = v^\alpha\iota_\alpha. \end{equation}

Technically, in view of the fact that we often identify

Given two vectors

\begin{equation}\notag v^a \in \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^p V^\alpha \quad \text{and} \quad w^b \in \bigoplus_{\beta=1}^q W^\beta, \end{equation}

their tensor product in

\begin{equation} \label{tensor-prod-eqn} \left( \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^p V^\alpha \right)\otimes \left(\bigoplus_{\beta=1}^q W^\beta \right) = \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^p \bigoplus_{\beta=1}^q (V^\alpha \otimes W^\beta) \end{equation}

will be written as $v^a w^b$, which is a short stand-in for the more precise expression

\begin{equation}\notag \sum_{\alpha=1}^p \sum_{\beta=1}^q v^\alpha\iota_\alpha \otimes w^\beta\iota_\beta. \end{equation}

Note that the right-hand side of \eqref{tensor-prod-eqn} is a double-indexed direct sum, instead of a single-indexed one like \eqref{linear2-eqn}, and that we’ve used two abstract indices to represent a vector in the former. More generally, it will be very convenient to consider arbitrary $p$-fold indexed direct sums of the form

\begin{equation}\notag \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^{m_1,\ldots,m_p} V^{\alpha_1\ldots\alpha_p} \overset{\text{def}}{=} \bigoplus_{\alpha_1=1}^{m_1} \cdots \bigoplus_{\alpha_p=1}^{m_p} V^{\alpha_1\ldots \alpha_p}, \end{equation}

and to write $p$ abstract indices for vectors in them, like $v^{a_1\ldots a_p}$. Then, given two vectors

\begin{equation}\notag v^{a_1\ldots a_p} \in \bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^{m_1,\ldots,m_p} V^{\alpha_1\ldots \alpha_p} \quad \text{and} \quad w^{b_1\ldots b_p} \in \bigoplus_{\beta=1}^{n_1,\ldots,n_q} W^{\beta_1\ldots \beta_q}, \end{equation}

their tensor product in

\begin{equation}\notag \left\{\bigoplus_{\alpha=1}^{m_1,\ldots,m_p} V^{\alpha_1\ldots \alpha_p}\right\} \otimes \left\{\bigoplus_{\beta=1}^{n_1,\ldots,n_q} W^{\beta_1\ldots \beta_q} \right\} = \bigoplus_{\alpha,\beta=1}^{m_1,\ldots,m_p,n_1,\ldots,n_q} \left(V^{\alpha_1\ldots \alpha_p} \otimes W^{\beta_1\ldots \beta_q} \right) \end{equation}

will be written as $v^{a_1\ldots a_p}w^{b_1\ldots b_q}$.
